question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, given a valid (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) tuple previously used to fill offer A, resubmit the identical ratifierData to fill offer A again in a second call to Midnight.take, because EcrecoverRatifier holds no per-fill nonce and isRootCanceled[maker][root] remains false, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind correct consumed state and prevent replay? Proof idea: fuzz (offer, units1, units2) where units1+units2 <= maxUnits, call take twice with identical ratifierData, assert consumed[maker][group] == units1+units2 and no extra credit/debt is minted beyond maker intent.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.hashOffer] Can an unprivileged taker take a different leaf offer from the same Merkle tree using the same root signature, because the signature covers only the root (not a specific leafIndex), and isLeaf accepts any valid (leafIndex, proof) pair for that root, allowing the maker's single signature to authorize all N offers in the tree simultaneously without per-offer consent? Proof idea: build a 4-leaf tree, sign root once, take all 4 distinct offers with the same sig and different (leafIndex, proof) pairs, assert all 4 fills succeed.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an authorized operator (any address for which isAuthorized[maker][operator] == true in Midnight) call cancelRoot(maker, root) to irreversibly cancel the maker's active offer root, causing all in-flight takes against that root to revert with RootCanceled, constituting a low-cost DoS of the maker's entire offer set? Proof idea: maker signs root, operator calls cancelRoot, assert subsequent take reverts; assert isRootCanceled is permanently true and cannot be unset.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can a malicious callback contract that the maker has authorized (isAuthorized[maker][callback] == true, required for maker callbacks in Midnight.take) call cancelRoot(maker, root) during the onBuy/onSell callback of an in-progress take, causing all subsequent partial fills of the same root to revert, while the current take already passed ratification and completes normally, creating a state where the maker's root is canceled mid-session? Proof idea: deploy callback that calls cancelRoot in onBuy, take with maker callback, assert root is canceled after take, assert second take reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker who obtains a valid (sig, root, proof) for offer O on chain A replay the same ratifierData on chain B (same chainid, different deployment of EcrecoverRatifier at a different address), because the domain separator includes address(this) and block.chainid, so cross-deployment replay is prevented — but does the domain separator correctly bind to the specific EcrecoverRatifier instance, and is there any path where two EcrecoverRatifier deployments share the same address (e.g., CREATE2 with same salt on different chains with same chainid)? Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverRatifier instances at different addresses, sign for one, assert the other rejects the signature.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier's EIP-712 domain omits 'name' and 'version' fields (using only chainId and verifyingContract), causing a wallet that enforces full EIP-712 domain display to show an incomplete domain to the maker, potentially leading the maker to sign a digest they believe is for a different protocol or version, violating the invariant that signatures must bind correct maker intent? Proof idea: compute domain separator with and without name/version, assert they differ, assert EcrecoverRatifier uses the shorter form.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash] Can an unprivileged taker manipulate the proof array length to select a different offerTreeTypeHash (e.g., submit proof.length=1 instead of proof.length=0 for a single-offer tree), causing the digest to be computed under a different typehash, and if the Merkle proof still passes isLeaf (e.g., by crafting a valid 2-leaf tree), does the signature verification accept a signature intended for a height-0 tree as valid for a height-1 tree? Proof idea: fuzz (proof.length, leafIndex) pairs, assert that a signature for height H cannot pass isRatified with a different height H' even if isLeaf passes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker pass a ratifierData where the proof array is empty (proof.length == 0) and leafIndex == 0, causing offerTreeTypeHash(0) to be used and the structHash to be keccak256(abi.encode(TYPEHASH_HEIGHT_0, root)), and if the maker signed this exact digest for a single-offer tree, does the ratifier correctly accept it — and conversely, does it correctly reject a proof.length=0 submission for an offer that was only ever part of a multi-offer tree? Proof idea: unit test both cases, assert acceptance/rejection.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit ratifierData with a valid signature but a leafIndex that has bits set beyond proof.length (e.g., leafIndex = 2^proof.length), causing HashLib.isLeaf to revert with LeafIndexOutOfRange rather than returning false, and does this revert propagate through Midnight.take as RatifierFail rather than InvalidProof, potentially confusing off-chain monitoring or error handling? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex values with high bits set, assert revert selector is consistent.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, after the maker's authorized signer (delegateSigner) has been revoked via Midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegateSigner, false, maker), still use a previously valid signature from delegateSigner to pass isRatified, because the authorization check IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer) is evaluated at take-time and correctly reflects the revoked state — confirming the revocation mechanism works — or is there a race condition where the revocation transaction and the take transaction are in the same block? Proof idea: revoke in same block as take, assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of maker A use their authorization to sign a Merkle root on behalf of maker A (since isAuthorized[makerA][attacker] == true allows attacker's signature to pass the isRatified check), effectively publishing offers in maker A's name without maker A's direct cryptographic consent, because the authorization model is coarse-grained and not scoped by action? Proof idea: authorize attacker, attacker signs root for makerA's offer, assert take succeeds with attacker's signature.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer whose offer.expiry == block.timestamp (boundary condition), where Midnight.take checks block.timestamp <= offer.expiry (passes), but the offer was signed as part of a root that the maker intended to expire at that exact timestamp — and does the EcrecoverRatifier provide any additional deadline enforcement beyond offer.expiry, or is the only time-based protection the offer.expiry field in the offer struct itself? Proof idea: set expiry = block.timestamp, assert take succeeds; set expiry = block.timestamp - 1, assert OfferExpired.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker use a valid (sig, root) pair where the signature was created by an authorized signer who was authorized AFTER the root was signed (i.e., the signer signed the root before being authorized, then was authorized, then the take is submitted), because isRatified checks authorization at take-time not at signing-time, allowing retroactive authorization to validate previously created signatures? Proof idea: sign root, then authorize signer, then take, assert success.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker who temporarily gains authorization (e.g., via a flash-loan-funded governance attack or a temporary setIsAuthorized grant) call cancelRoot for all of a maker's active roots, then lose authorization, leaving the maker's entire offer book permanently canceled with no recovery path, since isRootCanceled is a one-way flag with no unset mechanism? Proof idea: grant authorization, cancel all roots, revoke authorization, assert maker cannot re-enable canceled roots.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit ratifierData where the Signature struct has v=27 or v=28 with r=0 and s=0, causing ecrecover to return address(0), which is caught by require(_signer != address(0), InvalidSignature()), but does the error selector InvalidSignature() vs Unauthorized() differ from what Midnight.take expects (RatifierFail), and does this difference affect any off-chain system that parses revert reasons? Proof idea: submit zero signature, assert revert is InvalidSignature propagated as RatifierFail.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit a malleable signature (flipping s to secp256k1 order - s, flipping v) for a valid digest, causing ecrecover to return a different but valid address, and if that address happens to be an authorized signer for the maker, does the take succeed with a signature the maker never created? Proof idea: compute (v', r, s') = malleable form of valid sig, assert ecrecover returns different address, assert isRatified behavior.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.hashOffer] Can an unprivileged taker construct two distinct Offer structs O1 and O2 (differing in callbackData or other dynamic fields) that produce the same hashOffer output due to ABI encoding collisions in keccak256(offer.callbackData) or abi.encode packing, allowing a signature for O1 to be used to fill O2? Proof idea: fuzz (O1, O2) pairs with different callbackData lengths, assert hashOffer(O1) != hashOffer(O2) for all distinct offers.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.hashMarket] Can an unprivileged taker exploit a collision in hashMarket where two markets with different collateralParams arrays produce the same collateralParamsHash (due to the assembly keccak256 over the packed hashes array), allowing a signature for an offer in market M1 to be used for an offer in market M2 with different LLTV/oracle/collateral? Proof idea: fuzz collateralParams arrays of different lengths, assert hashMarket produces distinct outputs.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit ratifierData with proof.length > 20, causing HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash to revert with TreeTooHigh, which propagates through isRatified and Midnight.take as an unexpected revert (not RatifierFail), potentially causing issues for callers that only catch specific selectors? Proof idea: submit proof with length 21, assert revert selector is TreeTooHigh, assert Midnight.take reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, in a market where the maker's offer has offer.ratifier == address(ecrecoverRatifier) but isAuthorized[offer.maker][address(ecrecoverRatifier)] == false in Midnight, cause Midnight.take to revert with RatifierUnauthorized before isRatified is even called, meaning the ratifier's signature check is never reached — and does this create a DoS vector where an attacker front-runs the maker's setIsAuthorized transaction? Proof idea: submit take before maker authorizes ratifier, assert RatifierUnauthorized.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker use a valid signature for a root R that contains offer O with offer.maker == makerA, but submit the ratifierData in a take call where the Offer memory passed to isRatified has offer.maker == makerB (different from the signed offer), causing the Merkle proof to fail (since hashOffer includes maker), correctly preventing the attack — or is there any path where the offer passed to isRatified differs from the offer passed to Midnight.take? Proof idea: assert Midnight passes the exact same Offer struct to isRatified as received in take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit a valid (sig, root) for offer O in market M1, then use the same ratifierData to fill a structurally identical offer O' in market M2 (same offer fields except market), where hashOffer(O) != hashOffer(O') because hashMarket(M1) != hashMarket(M2), confirming cross-market replay is prevented — or does any field omission in hashMarket allow two distinct markets to collide? Proof idea: differential test hashMarket for all market field variations.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged attacker who is NOT authorized for maker call cancelRoot(maker, root) and have it succeed, because the authorization check is require(maker == msg.sender || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender)), and if isAuthorized returns true for any reason (e.g., a bug in Midnight's isAuthorized), the attacker can cancel any maker's roots? Proof idea: call cancelRoot as unauthorized address, assert Unauthorized revert; then authorize and assert success.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the EcrecoverRatifier has no per-offer or per-fill deadline in its signature scheme (unlike EcrecoverAuthorizer which has a deadline field in the Authorization struct), meaning a signed root remains valid indefinitely until explicitly canceled, allowing a taker to fill an offer years after the maker signed the root if the maker forgot to cancel it? Proof idea: sign root at T=0, warp to T=365 days, assert take still succeeds if root not canceled and offer.expiry is far future.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, given a Merkle tree where offer O has offer.expiry set to a past timestamp, still pass isRatified (which does not check expiry), but have the take revert at Midnight.take's require(block.timestamp <= offer.expiry, OfferExpired()) check — confirming the ratifier correctly delegates expiry enforcement to Midnight, and there is no path to bypass the expiry check via ratifier manipulation? Proof idea: set offer.expiry = block.timestamp - 1, sign root, assert take reverts with OfferExpired not RatifierFail.
